summary: Joel Spolsky argues that eating lunch together as a team is critically important for workplace culture and shouldn't be left to chance. He observes that even at companies with cafeterias like Google and Microsoft, many employees end up eating alone, which he sees as a failure. Drawing a parallel to the social dynamics of school cafeterias, he contends that communal meals are fundamental to human connection. At Fog Creek and Stack Exchange, he deliberately designed the lunch experience with long shared tables and expectations that newcomers join the group.
key_points: Even at companies with free or cheap cafeterias, many employees eat alone, often masking their discomfort with phones or taking food back to their desks
The social dynamics of workplace lunch mirror junior high school cafeteria politics, tapping into deep psychological needs around belonging
Intentional design choices like long tables instead of small round ones can structurally encourage communal eating
Fog Creek and Stack Exchange deliberately share meals across company boundaries despite being separate organizations
Building a great workplace requires treating social rituals like lunch as deliberate design decisions, not afterthoughts
money_quotes: I've been on teams that eat together every day, and it's awesome. I've been on teams that don't, and lunch every day is, at best, lonely.
Excuse me, I'd love to introduce myself to you, but it's very important that I update my cabbage.
The importance of eating together with your co-workers is not negotiable, to me. It's too important to be left to chance.
That's why we eat together at long tables, not a bunch of little round tables.
Eating together is a critical part of what it means to be human and what it means to have a humane workplace, and that's been a part of our values from day one.
There's a lot of stuff that's accidental about Fog Creek and Stack Exchange, but lunch is not one of them.
key_insight: Communal lunch is too important to workplace culture to be left to chance — it must be deliberately designed into the company's daily routine.
